For workout playlist tasks: after creating a playlist to meet a time-based requirement, verify the total duration exceeds the workout requirement by at least a small margin (e.g., 5-10 minutes) to account for potential timing variations. This ensures you won't run out of music during the workout even if some songs are shorter than expected or if the workout runs slightly longer.

For Venmo refund tasks where you need to refund the 'last' approved payment request: after identifying the most recent approved request by sorting all collected requests by created_at descending, verify it is indeed the most recent by checking that no other approved requests have a later created_at date. This ensures you refund the correct request on the first attempt, avoiding redundant transactions.

For tasks requiring following artists meeting specific criteria (genre, follower count), after following the artists identified through search, verify completeness by checking all artists (via paginated search without filters) to ensure no qualifying artists were missed due to search limitations or incomplete pagination. This ensures the task is fully completed even if the initial search results were incomplete.

For tasks involving filtering by numeric thresholds (e.g., follower count, play_count, like_count) where you use API parameters for server-side filtering, after completing the operation, verify completeness by performing a broader search without the numeric filter to collect all items in the category, then client-side filter by the threshold. This ensures no qualifying items were missed due to API limitations or incomplete pagination.

After sending payment requests for shared expenses, verify creation by fetching sent payment requests with pagination and checking that requests with the expected description and amounts exist for all intended recipients. This ensures no requests were missed due to API errors or network issues.

After parsing structured content from notes or messages, verify the extracted data by checking that the number of extracted items is reasonable (not zero when content clearly contains matches). If parsing yields empty results, immediately re-examine the raw format and adjust parsing strategy rather than proceeding with empty data. This prevents sending incorrect or empty responses based on flawed parsing.

After playing a song via play_music API (e.g., the most listened song from an album), verify playback by calling show_current_song API and checking that the song_id matches the intended song. This ensures the playback operation succeeded and the correct song is playing.

After rejecting/approving pending Venmo payment requests from specific relationship groups, verify completion by re-fetching all pending payment requests and checking that none remain from the target contacts. Filter by sender email matching the combined contact emails to confirm zero remaining requests. This ensures the batch operation was fully successful and no requests were missed.

After performing batch follow operations on artists derived from a source set (e.g., artists from liked songs), verify completeness by checking that the source artist IDs are a subset of the updated followed artist IDs using set operations: ensure set(source_artist_ids) ⊆ set(updated_followed_artist_ids). This provides a robust mathematical verification that all required artists are now followed.

After finding the 'most-liked' or similar maximum/minimum song across playlists, verify ties by checking if any other songs have the same metric value. If ties exist and the task asks for a singular answer (e.g., 'the title'), you may return any of the tied songs, but ensure your algorithm correctly handles ties by either returning the first encountered or explicitly noting the tie. This prevents incorrect results when multiple songs share the maximum/minimum value.

After performing batch operations (like liking transactions) on specific identified items, verify success efficiently by fetching only those items individually using APIs that accept transaction IDs (e.g., show_transaction). This avoids re-fetching entire collections (like social feed) which can be inefficient, especially when dealing with large datasets. For Venmo transactions, use show_transaction with each transaction_id to check final state metrics (like_count, comment_count).

Before performing irreversible actions (e.g., account termination, mass deletions), verify that backup files or critical outputs have been successfully created. Use file_exists or directory_exists APIs to confirm file existence before proceeding with destructive operations. This ensures the irreversible action only occurs after successful backup confirmation.

For backup/export tasks creating CSV files: after creating the CSV file, verify that the file contains the expected number of rows (excluding header) matching the count of unique items collected. Use file_system APIs to read the file and count lines, ensuring all items were written correctly. This ensures backup completeness before performing irreversible actions.

After creating CSV files with specific formatting requirements (e.g., artists separated by '|'), verify the formatting by reading a few lines and checking that artists are correctly joined with the specified separator. Ensure quotes are properly escaped and fields are formatted as expected. This prevents incorrect formatting that could render the backup unusable.

For backup/export tasks collecting data from multiple sources (e.g., song library, album library, playlists), verify that each source contributed songs by tracking counts per source (e.g., song library count, album song count, playlist song count) before union. This ensures completeness and confirms that data collection from all specified sources was successful.

After parsing structured content from notes or messages, verify that the extracted data matches the task requirements before sending or using it. For example, when extracting movie titles from a note for a recommendation request, check that: (1) the extracted titles match the filtering criteria (e.g., director name), (2) the number of extracted items is reasonable (not zero when content clearly contains matches), (3) the titles are correctly formatted (no extra whitespace or formatting issues). This prevents sending incorrect or empty responses based on flawed parsing.

After parsing structured note content for movie recommendations (or similar multi-line entries), verify the extracted titles match the raw content by manually inspecting a few entries and checking that filtering criteria (e.g., director name) are correctly applied. Ensure the number of extracted items is reasonable (not zero when content clearly contains matches).

When finding 'most frequent' or 'least frequent' items (e.g., most recommended artist) by counting occurrences across API data, after identifying the item with maximum/minimum count, verify ties by checking if any other item has the same count. This ensures correct handling of ambiguous cases where multiple items share the highest/lowest frequency. If ties exist and the task expects a single answer, either return any of the tied items or note the tie explicitly.

When parsing structured data (like notes, messages, lists) and initial parsing fails, re-examine the raw format closely to identify the exact pattern. Adjust parsing logic to match the observed structure—for example, if entries are separated by double newlines, split by delimiter rather than iterating lines with state tracking. Test parsing on a subset of data before applying to the entire dataset.

When parsing structured content with clear delimiters between entries (e.g., double newlines in notes), use delimiter-based splitting instead of line-by-line state tracking. This approach is more reliable and simpler for patterns where entries are separated by consistent delimiters.
Remember that the email addresses, access tokens and variables (e.g. spotify_password) in the example above are not valid anymore.

Once you have completed the task, make sure to call apis.supervisor.complete_task(). If the task asked for some information, return it as the answer argument, i.e. call apis.supervisor.complete_task(answer=<answer>). Many tasks do not require an answer, so in those cases, just call apis.supervisor.complete_task() i.e. do not pass any argument.
Always taste your dish at multiple stages of cooking. This practice allows you to adjust seasoning and flavors progressively, ensuring a balanced final result that meets your desired profile.

In sports coaching, prioritize individual skill assessments during practice sessions. Tailored feedback enhances player development by addressing specific strengths and weaknesses, leading to more effective training outcomes.

Automate savings transfers to a separate account immediately after each paycheck. This strategy reduces the temptation to spend excess funds and ensures consistent progress toward financial goals without requiring ongoing willpower.
